us-politics.headline: Trump Escalates Iran Threats, Refuses to Rule Out Ground Troops
us-politics.body:
President Trump spent Easter Sunday issuing profanity-laced threats against Iran on social media, warning he would strike power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. He declined to rule out deploying ground troops if a nuclear deal is not reached. The threats came as the US rescued a downed service member from behind enemy lines inside Iran, with CIA assistance in a deception operation.

Opinions on the strategy split sharply. A retired Army general predicted the conflict could end in under 60 days. A former Trump adviser warned the escalating rhetoric makes a deal less likely, not more. One House Republican said the only legitimate ground-troop mission would be seizing enriched uranium. A House Democrat argued Iran's grip on the Strait poses a greater strategic threat than its nuclear programme. The former World Bank chief said Iran's economy is already collapsing toward a barter system.

sources.title: Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Inside the daring rescue of airman behind enemy lines: How CIA assisted with 'deception campaign'
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict
Retired Army general predicts Iran war could wrap up in ‘less than 60 days’

us-politics.headline: NATO Fractures, Energy Costs Surge as Iran War Widens
us-politics.body:
The conflict with Iran is straining the Atlantic alliance. Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace signals a broader fracturing of NATO solidarity, with analysts describing a shift toward a fragmented, post-global alliance structure. Trump's pressure on NATO allies has deepened existing tensions within the bloc.

At home, the economic fallout is accelerating. Jet fuel costs have skyrocketed, forcing flight cancellations and fare hikes for travellers. Energy analysts argue the crisis exposes America's vulnerability to oil supply disruptions and makes the case for diversifying toward domestic and renewable energy sources. The White House, meanwhile, is separately pushing an ambitious moon-base agenda, framing space as the next strategic frontier.

sources.title: The war in Iran is showing why the US needs diverse energy resources
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
The White House vows America will never give up the moon again

us-politics.headline: Bondi Firing Rattles Washington; DHS Funding Lapses
us-politics.body:
Trump's dismissal of Attorney General Pam Bondi is generating bipartisan concern. Senator Tim Kaine called it a warning to whoever takes the job next, arguing the firing illustrates the political risks of serving in Trump's cabinet. NPR reporting framed the move alongside what it described as Trump walking back campaign commitments on law enforcement independence.

Separately, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries demanded Speaker Mike Johnson recall the House from recess after a lapse in DHS funding. Johnson has not responded publicly. Democrats are using the funding gap to press the case that Republican leadership is absent on basic government operations. A new ad campaign, backed by some Republicans, also launched this week opposing Trump's push to federalise election administration.

sources.title: Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Senate Democrat says Trump ‘needs a lot of prayer’
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace; Democrats Eye Affordability Trap
us-politics.body:
Newly elected Pope Leo used his first Easter address to deliver a forceful call for peace, speaking directly to a world marked by active conflicts including the US-Iran war. The address drew wide attention given its timing and the new pontiff's commanding tone.

On the domestic political front, analysts are warning that Democrats face a strategic risk on the economy. Despite hammering Republicans on cost-of-living issues, the party has struggled to offer concrete affordability policies, and some centrist observers argue that gap could cost them in the midterms. A federal judge this week also blocked a Trump administration effort to collect race-based college admissions data, adding another legal setback to the administration's higher-education agenda.

sources.title: ‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum
On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data

au-politics.headline: Logging of Swift Parrot Habitat Reignites Tasmanian Forest Debate
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest was logged after researchers recorded the call of the critically endangered swift parrot dozens of times within it. The bird is already under severe pressure from habitat loss, and conservationists argue the incident exposes failures in environmental protections meant to prevent exactly this outcome.

The case lands during a broader national conversation about land use and species protection. Critics of current forestry regulations say detection of an endangered species should trigger an automatic halt to logging. Industry and government have not publicly responded to this specific incident, but have previously defended existing approval processes as legally sound and scientifically informed.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

au-politics.headline: Court Orders Return of Aboriginal Child Moved 1,700km From Community
au-politics.body:
The Family Court has ruled that an Aboriginal child taken 1,700 kilometres from a remote Northern Territory community must be returned. The decision is a significant rebuke to whoever authorised the move, and raises fresh questions about child protection decisions affecting Indigenous families.

Aboriginal communities and advocates have long argued that removals of Indigenous children replicate the harms of historical policies. The ruling does not resolve the underlying child welfare concerns that prompted the move, and the case is likely to continue through the courts. It adds pressure on NT authorities to demonstrate that cultural connection and family ties are being properly weighed in child protection decisions.

sources.title: Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Pushes Used EV Prices Higher Across Australia
au-politics.body:
Australia's ongoing fuel crisis is reshaping the secondhand car market, with used electric vehicle prices rising as more buyers look to cut their exposure to volatile petrol costs. Sellers report stronger demand and shorter listing times, with some buyers citing the crisis as the tipping point in a decision they had been weighing for years.

The shift puts pressure on supply, since Australia's EV charging infrastructure and new vehicle stock remain uneven across states and regional areas. The trend benefits early EV adopters but risks leaving lower-income buyers behind, as affordable used EVs become harder to find. The government has not announced any emergency measures to address the supply gap.

sources.title: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

au-politics.headline: Police Negligence Lawsuit Looms After Officer Killed by Dezi Freeman
au-politics.body:
The sisters of a police officer killed by Dezi Freeman are preparing to sue the force for negligence, according to their lawyer. The case centres on whether police command made decisions that put the officer in foreseeable danger.

Freeman's name has been linked to prior incidents, and the family's legal team is expected to argue that systemic failures contributed to the officer's death. A successful negligence claim against a police force would be legally significant and could force a review of operational protocols. Police have not publicly commented on the planned lawsuit.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Crisis Grips Global Markets
world.body:
Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, sending shockwaves through European energy markets and pushing oil prices sharply higher. Trump threatened Iran with "hell" as a diplomatic deadline approached, but offered no concrete plan to reopen the waterway. Oman stepped in as a back-channel mediator, with Muscat announcing talks with Tehran on restoring transit. Iran's former foreign minister Zarif separately floated a peace roadmap, though Gulf states voiced deep skepticism.

The conflict's reach is already global. Nepal announced two-day weekends to conserve fuel. OPEC+ agreed to hike output but warned recovery would be slow. Analyst John Mearsheimer told Al Jazeera he sees no signs of a quick end to the fighting. Iran also struck an industrial area in southern Israel, while Israeli strikes killed at least 14 people across Lebanon.

sources.title: OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says

world.headline: Wounded US Airman Rescued from Inside Iran
world.body:
A seriously wounded US airman was rescued from inside Iranian territory after his F-15E was shot down, President Trump confirmed. The operation involved a covert extraction - details of which the BBC has partially reconstructed - marking one of the most dramatic American rescue missions in years. The airman was described as in serious but stable condition.

The rescue adds a human dimension to a conflict that is rapidly expanding in scope and risk. Separately, analysts and regional officials raised alarm over the potential consequences of strikes on Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant, warning that an attack could trigger a catastrophic radioactive release across the Gulf.

sources.title: How downed F-15 US airman was rescued inside Iran
Seriously wounded US airman rescued from Iran, Trump says
Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf
US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know

world.headline: Pope Leo Urges Peace at Easter as Lebanon Burns
world.body:
Newly elected Pope Leo used his first Easter Mass to call on world leaders to choose peace, warning that the world is growing dangerously indifferent to violence. The address landed as Christian families in Lebanon celebrated Easter while displaced from their homes by Israeli strikes. At least 14 people were killed across Lebanon in the past 24 hours.

Israel's national security minister Itamar Ben-Gvir also drew international condemnation after detailing plans to establish a Palestinian "death row," while footage emerged of an Israeli military dog attacking a Palestinian man inside a mosque.

sources.title: At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’
Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter
Pope Leo calls for global leaders to choose peace in his first Easter Mass
Footage shows Israeli military dog attacking Palestinian man inside mosque

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Photographs Moon as Bangladesh Measles Crisis Deepens
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew, including Canadian Jeremy Hansen - the first Canadian heading to the Moon - sent back striking photographs of Earth and the lunar surface, reigniting debate over the boundary between scientific documentation and spectacle. The mission marks a major milestone in the return of crewed lunar exploration.

On the ground, a suspected measles outbreak has killed nearly 100 children in Bangladesh, raising urgent questions about vaccination coverage. In Europe, Hungary alleged a plot to blow up a gas pipeline days before a national election, a claim that opposition figures quickly questioned. Ukraine struck a Russian oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod and a port in Primorsk, continuing its campaign against Russian energy infrastructure.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Earth in rear-view of Artemis II astronauts
Artemis's stunning Moon pictures - science or holiday photos?
Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh
Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon
Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

ai.headline: Suno's AI Music Tool Walks Into a Copyright Minefield
ai.body:
Suno, the AI music generation startup, is drawing fresh scrutiny over how its models handle copyrighted material. The Verge reports the tool can produce outputs that closely mimic existing artists and recordings, raising unresolved questions about liability that the music industry is watching closely.

The legal framework around AI-generated music remains largely untested. Suno has not secured blanket licensing deals with major labels, leaving users exposed if generated tracks surface in commercial contexts. The problem is structural, not incidental. As AI music tools scale, the gap between what they can produce and what they are legally permitted to reproduce is widening fast.

sources.title: Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: OpenAI Shifts Codex to Pay-Per-Use, Ending Free Access
ai.body:
OpenAI is moving Codex, its AI coding tool, to API-based pricing for all users. The change ends any remaining free tier and ties costs directly to usage volume, a shift that will hit hobbyists and small developers hardest.

The move mirrors a broader industry pattern: AI companies offer generous free access to build adoption, then tighten the economics once users are locked in. OpenAI published a rate card outlining the new pricing structure. Developers who built workflows around Codex now face a direct cost calculation that did not exist before.

sources.title: Codex is switching to API pricing based usage for all users

ai.headline: Japan's Labor Shortage Is Becoming a Robot Proving Ground
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots into roles that human workers have abandoned, from warehouse logistics to elder care. TechCrunch reports the country's acute demographic crunch is accelerating real-world adoption faster than anywhere else, turning Japan into an unplanned stress test for commercial robotics.

The framing matters. Where Western debates center on robots displacing workers, Japan's situation is the inverse - there are not enough workers to displace. That changes the political calculus entirely and may explain why public resistance is lower. If the deployments hold up, Japan's data will become the benchmark other markets use to justify their own rollouts.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Gemini Enters Google Maps, SpaceX Eyes Orbital Data Centers
ai.body:
Google has begun integrating Gemini into Maps, letting users hand off day planning to the AI assistant. The Verge tested it and found the experience genuinely useful, with Gemini pulling together routes, venues, and timing into coherent itineraries rather than just answering isolated queries. It is a concrete consumer use case at scale.

Meanwhile, TechCrunch reports that SpaceX is exploring orbital data centers as a potential revenue stream, one that analysts suggest could help support the company's enormous private valuation. The idea is early-stage, but it signals that space infrastructure ambitions are expanding well beyond launch services and Starlink connectivity.

sources.title: Can orbital data centers help justify a massive valuation for SpaceX?
I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well

btc.headline: North Korea Behind $270M Drift Protocol Heist
btc.body:
The Drift Protocol exploit was not a smash-and-grab. According to post-incident findings, North Korean intelligence operatives spent six months infiltrating the platform before draining roughly $270-280 million in a coordinated attack. The operation involved deliberate preparation across multiple stages, suggesting state-level resources and patience.

The breach adds to a growing ledger of DPRK-linked crypto thefts, which analysts say fund the regime's weapons programs. Ledger's CTO separately warned this week that AI is accelerating the threat, giving attackers better tools to probe vulnerabilities, craft phishing campaigns, and automate exploits at scale. The security window for crypto protocols is narrowing fast.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K as Sentiment Turns Deeply Bearish
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 and traders are losing patience. Sentiment has hit its worst levels since the Iran war began, yet price has not collapsed - a divergence that some read as resilience and others read as a coiled spring waiting to break lower. At least one prominent trader argues new lows are a matter of time.

The macro backdrop is shifting beneath the market. CoinDesk reports that Bitcoin is now front-running Federal Reserve decisions rather than reacting to them, a behavioral change driven by institutional ETF flows that have made BTC more sensitive to rate expectations. Meanwhile, a Bitcoin Policy Institute executive framed BTC and the US dollar as symbiotic rather than adversarial, pushing back on the narrative that one must destroy the other.

sources.title: Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec

btc.headline: Token Oversupply Threatens Crypto's Long-Term Value Case
btc.body:
A structural problem is quietly undermining the broader crypto market: token supply is growing faster than the value those tokens represent. New issuance from vesting schedules, protocol launches, and incentive programs keeps hitting the market, diluting holders and suppressing prices even when fundamentals look reasonable. Critics call it an existential issue.

Robert Kiyosaki resurfaced this week to argue that Bitcoin and gold remain the answer, drawing a line from the 1974 decoupling of the dollar from hard assets to today's inflationary environment. His framing is familiar, but the timing lands against a backdrop of genuine structural concern about whether most crypto tokens can sustain any value at all.

sources.title: Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle
‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation

btc.headline: Ant Group Builds Crypto Rails for AI Agents to Transact
btc.body:
Ant Group's blockchain division has launched a platform designed to let AI agents conduct transactions on crypto infrastructure autonomously. The system targets use cases where software agents need to pay for services, settle agreements, or move value without human sign-off at each step. It is an early but concrete bet that AI and crypto converge at the payments layer.

The move arrives as Anthropic launched a political action committee amid growing tensions with the Trump administration over AI regulation. Neither development is purely a crypto story, but both signal that the boundary between AI infrastructure and blockchain rails is collapsing faster than most of the industry anticipated.

sources.title: Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
Anthropic launches PAC amid tensions with Trump administration over AI policy

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart with caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms lacquered in white miso butter on a flaky rough-puff pastry base.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry for 8 minutes before adding the filling to prevent a soggy bottom — the miso glaze releases moisture as it cooks.

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks at progressively faster paces — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and back to moderate — training your body to recover at speed rather than at rest.

learn.topic: Eigenvalues and the Google PageRank Algorithm
learn.description: PageRank, the algorithm that made Google dominant, is essentially an application of a linear algebra concept called the dominant eigenvector — it treats the entire web as a matrix and finds which pages the system naturally 'settles on' as important.
learn.why: Understanding this collapses the mystery behind how recommendation engines, social network influence scores, and even epidemiological spread models actually work — it's one idea that quietly runs much of the modern world.
